segments.text: This is the most detailed map of dark
matter we've ever made. Dark matter
makes up 27% of our universe, but we
don't really understand it because it's
invisible, but it does have mass. So, it
pulls on things like stars and galaxies,
warping the light that they emit. And
using the James Web Space Telescope, we
can measure that warping and fill in the
blank spots. That's the dark matter.
This is the previous map that we made
with the Hubble telescope in 2007. And
this is the new map made by Web. This is
it overlaid onto the stars and galaxies.
So, you can see the scale here. There
are giant globs of dark matter that we
couldn't ever see. Now with web's higher
resolution, we can see more of the
spider-like details of this dark matter.
These two clusters are now more
distinct, and this one is denser because
we could reduce the noise in the data.
And this one's totally new. Clear
pictures of dark matter are really
important for understanding what the
heck is going on in our galaxy. So if
you need more optimistic science and
tech stories, subscribe.
